title: Oscillatory photoresistance on the high field side of the cyclotron resonance
abstract: We consider the displacement contribution to photoresistance in overlapping Landau levels at radiation frequencies much smaller than the cyclotron frequency. We show that in the limit of short-range disorder and high radiation power, this contribution leads to a new class of magneto-resistance oscillations. These oscillations, which we call radiowave-induced resistance oscillations (RIROs), are distinct from the well known microwave-induced resistance oscillations in the following aspects: (i) their amplitude is independent of power, (ii) their period is controlled by the radiation electric field, rather than by the radiation frequency, and (iii) they can be either $1/B$ or $1/B^2$-periodic, depending on $B$, with the crossover point linked to the width of the cyclotron resonance absorption curve. We also show that RIROs should be readily observed in experiments.
summary: This paper predicts a new type of resistance oscillation in 2D electron systems called RIROs, which occur at low frequencies and high power. Unlike standard microwave-induced oscillations, these are driven by the radiation electric field strength rather than the frequency. This discovery provides a potential new method for mapping cyclotron resonance and measuring electric fields in semiconductor heterostructures.
main_problem: The paper investigates a new class of magneto-resistance oscillations, termed radiowave-induced resistance oscillations (RIROs), occurring on the high-field side of the cyclotron resonance.
main_result: The author identifies that RIROs are distinct from MIROs because their amplitude is power-independent and their period is controlled by the radiation electric field rather than the radiation frequency.
method: The study uses an analytical derivation based on the displacement contribution to photoresistance, examining the limit of high radiation power and low radiation frequency.
model_or_system: The physical system is a two-dimensional electron system (2DES) in the regime of overlapping Landau levels with short-range disorder.
key_observables: Oscillatory photoresistance (RIROs), radiation electric field (E), and cyclotron resonance absorption width.
important_parameters: Radiation frequency (omega), cyclotron frequency (omega_c), radiation electric field (E), radiation power (P), and the Dingle factor.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the limit of short-range/sharp disorder, low radiation frequency (omega << omega_c), and high radiation power (P >> 1).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows dimensionless microwave power versus magnetic field for various frequencies and polarizations; Figure 2 shows calculated photoresistance versus magnetic field for different frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the phenomenon of RIROs, provides a theoretical derivation of the photoresistivity, compares the results to known MIROs, analyzes the periodicity regimes (1/B vs 1/B^2), and discusses experimental observability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Dimensionless microwave power P as a function of mag- netic ﬁeld B, computed using Eq. (3), for f = 1.00, 10.0, and 100 GHz, as marked. Solid lines correspond to linear polarization, Eq. (3), and dashed (dotted) lines to circular active (inactive) polar- izations. Dotted vertical lines mark the position of the cyclotron reso- nance (CR) ωc = ω. Dashed vertical line is drawn at B = m⋆/eτcr.

FIG. 2. Photoresistance ρ/ρD as a function of magnetic ﬁeld B calculated using Eq. (1) for (a) f = 1.00 GHz, (b) 10.0 GHz, and (c) 100 GHz, as marked [35]. Solid lines correspond to linear polar- ization, Eq. (3), and dashed (dotted) are the results for circular active (inactive) polarizations. Dotted vertical lines mark the position of the cyclotron resonance (CR) ωc = ω. Dashed vertical line is drawn at B = m⋆/eτcr.

FIG. 3. Parameter ξ, Eq. (2), (solid line, left axis) and ǫ = ω/ωc (dashed line, right axis) as a function of magnetic ﬁeld B calculated for f = 1.00 GHz. Dashed vertical line is drawn at B = m⋆/eτcr.

FIG. 4. (a) Photoresistivity ρ/ρD as a function of magnetic ﬁeld B calculated using Eq. (1) (solid lines) and Eq. (6) (dashed lines) for f = 1.00 GHz (and for f = 0.10 GHz) [35]. The microwave ﬁeld is E = 250 V/m. As expected, the curves corresponding to different f are nearly indistinguishable. (b) Photoresistivity ρ/ρD as a function of magnetic ﬁeld B calculated using Eq. (1) (solid line) and Eq. (6) (dashed line) for f = 1.00 GHz and E = 125 V/m [35].
